perturbation.original_text: One party hereto ("Owner") may disclose to the other party hereto ("Recipient") certain Confidential Information pursuant to this Agreement which Owner deems proprietary and confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: One party hereto ("Owner") may disclose to the other party hereto ("Recipient") certain Confidential Information pursuant to this Agreement which Owner deems proprietary, but not confidential.
perturbation.explanation: By stating the information is proprietary but not confidential, it creates a contradiction. Proprietary information often relies on confidentiality to maintain its value. Removing the confidentiality aspect affects its protection.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426)
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act defines a trade secret based on its confidential nature. Removing the 'confidential' aspect weakens the argument for trade secret protection under California law. A trade secret relies on not being generally known to the public.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Title 5. Uniform Trade Secrets Act :: Civil Code :: 2010 California Code :: California Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the California Code 2024 California Code 2023 California Code 2022 California Code 2010 California Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2010 California Code Civil Code Title 5. Uniform Trade Secrets Act CIVIL CODE SECTION 3426-3426.11 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) "Misappropriation" means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) Before a material change of his or her position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (c) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, limited liability company, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (d) "Trade secret" means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process, that: (1) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to the public or to other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (2) Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances t
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the term 'confidential' from the description of information disclosed by the Owner to the Recipient. According to the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation, confidentiality is crucial for trade secret protection under California law. The 'California Code, CIV 3426' snippets refer to the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, which implies the importance of maintaining secrecy. By stating the information is proprietary but not confidential, the changed text undermines the basis for trade secret protection, creating a legal contradiction related to the necessary conditions for protecting proprietary information under trade secret law.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties agree that Recipient shall not use and shall prevent the disclosure of any information it receives from Owner that is marked PROPRIETARY AND CONFIDENTIAL, or similarly marked, or any other information (whether delivered in writing or verbally) which by its nature would be reasonably considered as confidential, to any other person, firm or corporation or delivered in connection with the evaluation of the Transaction, except as provided herein, and shall use the same degree of care to avoid disclosure of such information as Recipient employs with respect to its own Confidential Information of like importance.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties agree that Recipient shall not use and shall prevent the disclosure of any information it receives from Owner that is marked PROPRIETARY, or similarly marked, or any other information (whether delivered in writing or verbally) which by its nature would be reasonably considered business intelligence, to any other person, firm or corporation or delivered in connection with the evaluation of the Transaction, except as provided herein, and shall use a reasonable degree of care to avoid disclosure of such information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Confidential Information' to 'business intelligence' reduces the legal protections and shifts the standard of care. Business intelligence may not be legally protected to the same extent as Confidential Information. Changing 'the same degree of care' to 'a reasonable degree of care' makes the standard of care less strict.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426)
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act protects trade secrets. By changing 'Confidential Information' to 'business intelligence' and lessening the standard of care, it makes it more difficult to argue for trade secret protection under California law.

Uniform Trade Secrets Act CIVIL CODE SECTION 3426-3426.11 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) "Misappropriation" means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) Before a material change of his or her position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (c) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, limited liability company, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (d) "Trade secret" means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process, that: (1) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to the public or to other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (2) Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances t
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the protection of trade secrets, potentially contradicting the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act. By changing 'Confidential Information' to 'business intelligence' and reducing the standard of care from 'the same degree of care' to 'a reasonable degree of care,' the modified text makes it more difficult to argue for trade secret protection. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines 'trade secret' as information that 'Derives independent economic value...from not being generally known' and 'is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy.' Reducing the standard of care weakens the 'efforts...to maintain its secrecy,' potentially failing to meet the legal definition of a trade secret, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be construed, interpreted, and enforced pursuant to the laws and judicial precedents of the State of California, without reference to principles of conflicts of law.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be construed, interpreted, and enforced pursuant to principles of equity and good conscience, without reference to any law.
perturbation.explanation: Removing reference to California law and replacing it with 'principles of equity and good conscience' creates legal uncertainty. Contracts are generally enforced under established legal principles, not subjective notions of equity.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1636 states a contract must be interpreted to give effect to the mutual intention of the parties as it existed at the time of contracting, so far as the same is ascertainable and lawful. Disregarding state laws in its construction contradicts this, leading to uncertainty in enforcement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1636 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1636. A contract must be so interpreted as to give effect to the mutual intention of the parties as it existed at the time of contracting, so far as the same is ascertainable and lawful. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces the application of California law with 'principles of equity and good conscience,' fundamentally contradicting the original agreement to be enforced under California law. California Civil Code § 1636, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, requires a contract to be interpreted to give effect to the mutual intention of the parties as it existed at the time of contracting, so far as the same is ascertainable and lawful. Removing the reference to state law undermines this principle, potentially leading to interpretations inconsistent with the parties' original intentions under established legal frameworks.

perturbation.original_text: Neither Party makes any representations or warranties as to the accuracy or completeness of the Confidential Information and neither Party shall have any liability to the other resulting from any use of the Confidential Information which is consistent with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Neither Party makes any representations or warranties as to the commercial value of the Disclosed Information and neither Party shall have any liability to the other resulting from any use of the Disclosed Information which is contemplated by this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'accuracy or completeness of the Confidential Information' to 'commercial value of the Disclosed Information' and 'consistent' to 'contemplated' narrows the scope of protection. A party may still have liability for inaccurate information even if it has commercial value. 'Contemplated' also implies that the party can do anything as long as it is inside the original intent.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code § 2313 outlines the creation of express warranties by the seller. By disclaiming warranties related to the 'commercial value' rather than the 'accuracy' or 'completeness' of the information, the contract attempts to evade potential liabilities associated with warranties implied under the UCC, potentially conflicting with consumer protection laws.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 2313. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 2. SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract [2301 - 2328] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2313. (1) Express warranties by the seller are created as follows: (a) Any affirmation of fact or promise made by the seller to the buyer which relates to the goods and becomes part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the goods shall conform to the affirmation or promise. (b) Any description of the goods which is made part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the goods shall conform to the description. (c) Any sample or model which is made part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the whole of the goods shall conform to the sample or model. (2) It is not necessary to the creation of an express warranty that the seller use formal words such as “warrant” or “guarantee” or that he have a specific intention to make a warranty, but an affirmation merely of the value of the goods or a statement purporting to be merely the seller’s opinion or commendation of the goods does not create a warranty. (Enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819.)
perturbation.justification: The modification changes the scope of the disclaimer from 'accuracy or completeness of the Confidential Information' to 'commercial value of the Disclosed Information.' The original text disclaims liability for any use of the confidential information that is consistent with the agreement, which is a much broader protection. The modified text disclaims warranties related to 'commercial value' rather than 'accuracy' or 'completeness,' which, according to the Law Explanation, attempts to evade potential liabilities associated with warranties implied under the UCC, potentially conflicting with consumer protection laws. This aligns with the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' because the contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, specifically concerning express warranties. The altered language weakens the protection against potential liabilities associated with the accuracy and completeness of the information, which is a core element of the original agreement. Scraped Law Snippet 1, COM 2313, states that any affirmation of fact or promise made by the seller to the buyer which relates to the goods and becomes part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the goods shall conform to the affirmation or promise. By limiting the disclaimer to commercial value, the modified text attempts to circumvent this provision, creating a legal contradiction.
